name: Naga
description: Nagas are serpentine or semi-divine beings, prominent in Hindu, Buddhist, and Jain mythology. They are often depicted as powerful and wise, possessing magical abilities and guarding precious treasures. Nagas can be benevolent protectors or malevolent adversaries, depending on the specific story and context. They are associated with water, fertility, and the earth, and are often depicted in connection with rivers, lakes, and the underworld. Some Nagas are considered to be divine beings, while others are seen as monstrous creatures. Their forms can vary, ranging from completely serpentine to having a human upper body and a serpent's lower body. They are often depicted with multiple heads and adorned with jewels. The Naga king, Vasuki, is famously used as a rope to churn the ocean of milk in the Samudra Manthana narrative. Nagas play a significant role in many Hindu stories and are revered in temples and rituals. Their images are often found in temples, guarding deities and sacred spaces, symbolizing protection and power. Nagas are deeply intertwined with the cultural and religious landscape of India and other regions influenced by Hinduism and Buddhism.
subtitle: Serpentine beings of Hindu lore
appearance: Nagas typically have a serpentine body, often with multiple heads. Some are depicted with a human upper body and a snake's lower body. They are often adorned with jewels and have scales that shimmer with various colors. Their eyes are piercing and hypnotic, and their fangs are venomous. The size of Nagas can vary greatly, from small, snake-like creatures to enormous, powerful beings.
habitat: Nagas are often associated with water, living in rivers, lakes, oceans, and underground realms. They are also found in forests, caves, and mountains. They are believed to inhabit the underworld, known as Patala, which is a realm of great wealth and power.
behavior: Nagas can be both benevolent and malevolent. Benevolent Nagas are protectors of dharma, guardians of treasures, and bestowers of blessings. Malevolent Nagas can be wrathful, vengeful, and dangerous, causing floods, droughts, and diseases. Their behavior often depends on their individual nature and the circumstances surrounding them. They are generally considered to be intelligent and wise, possessing knowledge of the past, present, and future.

keyMyths.title: Samudra Manthana
keyMyths.description: Vasuki, the Naga king, is used as a rope to churn the ocean of milk, which results in the emergence of Amrita (the nectar of immortality). This event is a crucial part of Hindu cosmology and mythology.

keyMyths.title: Kaliya's Defeat
keyMyths.description: Lord Krishna dances on the head of the venomous Naga Kaliya to subdue him and protect the villagers from his poisonous fumes. Kaliya eventually submits to Krishna's power and is banished from the Yamuna River.

sources.author: Wendy Doniger
sources.description: A comprehensive overview of Hindu mythology, including various Naga stories.
sources.text: A comprehensive overview of Hindu mythology, including various Naga stories.

sources.author: Devdutt Pattanaik
